A Classifier is a machine learning model that learns to categorize input data into predefined classes or labels.

![][image1]

To train an image classifier, we need the steps below:

1. Load and normalize the CIFAR10 training and test datasets using torchvision.  
2. Define a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN).  
   1. A type of deep learning model designed primarily for processing grid-like data such as images.  
3. Define a loss function.  
4. Train the network on the training data.  
5. Test the network on the test data.

We start with step 1\.  
Using torchvision, it is extremely easy to load CIFAR10.

| import torch import torchvision from torchvision.transforms import v2 |
| :---- |

The output of torchvision datasets are PILImage images of range \[0, 1\]. We transform them to Tensors of a normalized range \[-1, 1\].

| [transform](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.transforms.v2.Compose.html#torchvision.transforms.v2.Compose) \= [v2.Compose](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.transforms.v2.Compose.html#torchvision.transforms.v2.Compose)(\[     [v2.ToImage](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.transforms.v2.ToImage.html#torchvision.transforms.v2.ToImage)(),     [v2.ToDtype](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.transforms.v2.ToDtype.html#torchvision.transforms.v2.ToDtype)([torch.float32](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensor_attributes.html#torch.dtype), scale=True),     [v2.Normalize](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.transforms.v2.Normalize.html#torchvision.transforms.v2.Normalize)((0.5, 0.5, 0.5), (0.5, 0.5, 0.5))\]) batch\_size \= 4 [trainset](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.datasets.CIFAR10.html#torchvision.datasets.CIFAR10) \= [torchvision.datasets.CIFAR10](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.datasets.CIFAR10.html#torchvision.datasets.CIFAR10)(root='./data', train=True,                                   download=True, [transform](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.transforms.v2.Compose.html#torchvision.transforms.v2.Compose)\=[transform](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.transforms.v2.Compose.html#torchvision.transforms.v2.Compose)) [trainloader](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/data.html#torch.utils.data.DataLoader) \= [torch.utils.data.DataLoader](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/data.html#torch.utils.data.DataLoader)([trainset](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.datasets.CIFAR10.html#torchvision.datasets.CIFAR10),                        batch\_size=batch\_size,shuffle=True, num\_workers=2) [testset](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.datasets.CIFAR10.html#torchvision.datasets.CIFAR10) \= [torchvision.datasets.CIFAR10](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.datasets.CIFAR10.html#torchvision.datasets.CIFAR10)(root='./data', train=False,                                   download=True, [transform](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.transforms.v2.Compose.html#torchvision.transforms.v2.Compose)\=[transform](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.transforms.v2.Compose.html#torchvision.transforms.v2.Compose)) [testloader](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/data.html#torch.utils.data.DataLoader) \= [torch.utils.data.DataLoader](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/data.html#torch.utils.data.DataLoader)([testset](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.datasets.CIFAR10.html#torchvision.datasets.CIFAR10),                    batch\_size=batch\_size,shuffle=False, num\_workers=2) classes \= ('plane', 'car', 'bird', 'cat',            'deer', 'dog', 'frog', 'horse', 'ship', 'truck') |
| :---- |

We can then show some of the training images

| import matplotlib.pyplot as plt import numpy as np \# functions to show an image  def imshow(img):     img \= img / 2 \+ 0.5     \# unnormalize     npimg \= img.numpy()     plt.imshow(np.transpose(npimg, (1, 2, 0)))     plt.show() \# get some random training images dataiter \= iter([trainloader](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/data.html#torch.utils.data.DataLoader)) [images](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor), [labels](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor) \= next(dataiter) \# show images imshow([torchvision.utils.make\_grid](https://docs.pytorch.org/vision/stable/generated/torchvision.utils.make_grid.html#torchvision.utils.make_grid)([images](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor))) \# print labels print(' '.join(f'{classes\[[labels](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor)\[j\]\]:5s}' for j in range(batch\_size))) |
| :---- |

The output then shows:

| ![][image2] bird   cat   car   deer |
| :---- |

Step 2: Define a CNN

We can copy the neural network from the NN section before and modify it to take 3-channel images (instead of 1-channel images as it was previously defined).

| import torch.nn as nn import torch.nn.functional as F class Net([nn.Module](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.Module.html#torch.nn.Module)):     def \_\_init\_\_(self):         super().\_\_init\_\_()         self.conv1 \= [nn.Conv2d](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.Conv2d.html#torch.nn.Conv2d)(3, 6, 5)         self.pool \= [nn.MaxPool2d](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.MaxPool2d.html#torch.nn.MaxPool2d)(2, 2)         self.conv2 \= [nn.Conv2d](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.Conv2d.html#torch.nn.Conv2d)(6, 16, 5)         self.fc1 \= [nn.Linear](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.Linear.html#torch.nn.Linear)(16 \* 5 \* 5, 120)         self.fc2 \= [nn.Linear](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.Linear.html#torch.nn.Linear)(120, 84)         self.fc3 \= [nn.Linear](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.Linear.html#torch.nn.Linear)(84, 10)     def forward(self, x):         x \= self.pool([F.relu](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.functional.relu.html#torch.nn.functional.relu)(self.conv1(x)))         x \= self.pool([F.relu](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.functional.relu.html#torch.nn.functional.relu)(self.conv2(x)))         x \= [torch.flatten](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.flatten.html#torch.flatten)(x, 1) \# flatten all dimensions except batch         x \= [F.relu](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.functional.relu.html#torch.nn.functional.relu)(self.fc1(x))         x \= [F.relu](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.functional.relu.html#torch.nn.functional.relu)(self.fc2(x))         x \= self.fc3(x)         return x net \= [Net](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.Module.html#torch.nn.Module)() |
| :---- |

For the third step, we have to create a loss function and optimizer.  
We can use a Classification Cross-Entropy loss and SGD with momentum:

| import torch.optim as optim criterion \= nn.CrossEntropyLoss() optimizer \= optim.SGD(net.parameters(), lr \= 0.001, momentum \= 0.9) |
| :---- |

Next, we can train the network.  
We simply have to loop over our data iterator, and feed the inputs to the network and optimize.

| Input | for epoch in range(2): \# loop over the dataset multiple times      running\_loss \= 0.0     for i, data in enumerate([trainloader](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/data.html#torch.utils.data.DataLoader), 0):         \# get the inputs; data is a list of \[inputs, labels\]         [inputs](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor), [labels](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor) \= data         \# zero the parameter gradients         [optimizer.zero\_grad](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.optim.SGD.html#torch.optim.SGD.zero_grad)()         \# forward \+ backward \+ optimize         [outputs](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor) \= net([inputs](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor))         [loss](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor) \= [criterion](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss.html#torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss)([outputs](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor), [labels](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor))         [loss.backward](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.Tensor.backward.html#torch.Tensor.backward)()         [optimizer.step](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.optim.SGD.html#torch.optim.SGD.step)()         \# print statistics         running\_loss \+= [loss](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor).item()         if i % 2000 \== 1999: \# print every 2000 mini-batches             print(f'\[{epoch \+ 1}, {i \+ 1:5d}\] loss: {running\_loss / 2000:.3f}')             running\_loss \= 0.0 print('Finished Training') |
| :---- | :---- |
| Output | \[1,  2000\] loss: 2.195 \[1,  4000\] loss: 1.825 \[1,  6000\] loss: 1.626 \[1,  8000\] loss: 1.552 \[1, 10000\] loss: 1.482 \[1, 12000\] loss: 1.454 \[2,  2000\] loss: 1.377 \[2,  4000\] loss: 1.375 \[2,  6000\] loss: 1.328 \[2,  8000\] loss: 1.300 \[2, 10000\] loss: 1.304 \[2, 12000\] loss: 1.283 Finished Training |

We can quickly save our trained model:

| PATH \= ‘./cifar\_net.pt’ torch.save(net.state\_dict(), PATH) |
| :---- |

Lastly, for step 5, we can test the network on the test data.

So far, we have trained the network for 2 passes over the training dataset. But, we should check if the network has learned anything at all.  
We will check this by predicting the class label that the neural network outputs, and checking it against the ground-truth. If the prediction is correct, we add the sample to the list of correct predictions.

First, let us display an image from the test set to get familiar.

| dataiter \= iter(testloader) images, labels \= next(dataiter) \# print images imshow(torchvision.utils.make\_grid(images)) print(‘GroundTruth: ’, ‘ ’.join(f’{classes\[labels\[j\]\]:5s}’ for j in range(4))) |
| :---- |

The output then shows:

| ![][image3] GroundTruth:  cat   ship   ship   plane |
| :---- |

Next, we can load it back into our saved model

| Input | net \= Net() net.load\_state\_dict(toch.load(PATH, weights\_only \= True)) |
| :---- | :---- |
| Output | \<All keys matched successfully\> |

Now, let us see what the neural network thinks these examples above are:

| outputs \= net(images) |
| :---- |

The outputs are energies for the ten classes. The higher the energy for a class, the more the network thinks that the image is of the particular class. So, let’s get the index of the highest energy (think of energy as a confidence value).

| Input | \_, predicted \= torch.max(outputs, 1\) print(‘Predicted: ‘, ‘ ’.join(f’{classes\[predicted\[j\]\]:5s’} for j in range(4))) |
| :---- | :---- |
| Output | Predicted:   cat   car   ship   car |

Let us look at how the network performs on the whole dataset.

| Input | correct \= 0 total \= 0 \# since we're not training, we don't need to calculate the gradients for our outputs with [torch.no\_grad](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.no_grad.html#torch.no_grad)():     for data in [testloader](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/data.html#torch.utils.data.DataLoader):         [images](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor), [labels](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor) \= data         \# calculate outputs by running images through the network         [outputs](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor) \= net([images](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor))         \# the class with the highest energy is what we choose as prediction         [\_](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor), [predicted](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor) \= [torch.max](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.max.html#torch.max)([outputs](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor), 1)         total \+= [labels](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor).size(0)         correct \+= ([predicted](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor) \== [labels](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor)).sum().item() print(f'Accuracy of the network on the 10000 test images: {100 \* correct // total} %') |
| :---- | :---- |
| Output | Accuracy of the network on the 10000 test images: 55 % |

We can see that the model performs way better than just choosing a random class (10%, as there are 10 classes). It seems like the network has learned something.

We can analyze what classes performed well and what classes didn’t:

| Input | \# prepare to count predictions for each class correct\_pred \= {classname: 0 for classname in classes} total\_pred \= {classname: 0 for classname in classes} \# again no gradients needed with [torch.no\_grad](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.no_grad.html#torch.no_grad)():     for data in [testloader](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/data.html#torch.utils.data.DataLoader):         [images](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor), [labels](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor) \= data         [outputs](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor) \= net([images](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor))         [\_](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor), [predictions](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor) \= [torch.max](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.max.html#torch.max)([outputs](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor), 1)         \# collect the correct predictions for each class         for [label](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor), [prediction](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor) in zip([labels](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor), [predictions](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor)):             if [label](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor) \== [prediction](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor):                 correct\_pred\[classes\[[label](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor)\]\] \+= 1             total\_pred\[classes\[[label](https://docs.pytorch.org/docs/stable/tensors.html#torch.Tensor)\]\] \+= 1 \# print accuracy for each class for classname, correct\_count in correct\_pred.items():     accuracy \= 100 \* float(correct\_count) / total\_pred\[classname\]     print(f'Accuracy for class: {classname:5s} is {accuracy:.1f} %') |
| :---- | :---- |
| Output | Accuracy for class: plane is 61.8 % Accuracy for class: car   is 75.3 % Accuracy for class: bird  is 42.1 % Accuracy for class: cat   is 27.6 % Accuracy for class: deer  is 42.5 % Accuracy for class: dog   is 65.3 % Accuracy for class: frog  is 36.9 % Accuracy for class: horse is 66.2 % Accuracy for class: ship  is 72.3 % Accuracy for class: truck is 61.1 % |

How do we run these neural networks on the GPU?

Just like how you transfer a tensor onto the GPU, you transfer the neural net onto the GPU.  
Let us first define our device as the first visible cuda device if we have CUDA available.

Note that the rest of this section assumes that the device is a CUDA device.

| Input | Device \= torch.device(torch.accelerator.current\_accelerator().type if torch.accelerator.is\_avaliable() else ‘cpu’) \# assuming that we are on a CUDA machine, this should print a CUDA device: print(device) |
| :---- | :---- |
| Output | cuda |

Then these methods will recursively go over all modules and convert their parameters and buffers to CUDA tensors:

| net.to(device) |
| :---- |

Remember that you will have to send the inputs and targets at every step to the GPU too:

| inputs, labels \= data\[0\].to(device), data\[1\].to(device) |
| :---- |

NOTE: both the network and its inputs MUST be on the same device. Otherwise, it will crash.

[image1]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image2]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image3]: <data:image/png;base64,>
